purpose: Extends the original 5+3 sector framework (sector_and_policy_recommendations_2026-07-18.json) to 10 priority sectors, and for each identifies specific target FDI-source countries with sourced, dated evidence -- not a generic 'attract FDI' list. Adds a game-theoretic frame for the tariff-rate-quota dynamics now shaping several of these sectors, and a dated timeline of OFFICIAL (non-speculative) tariff announcements through the retrieval date.
method: Multi-agent research across 10 sectors, each cross-checked against at least one primary/official source (PIB, ministry press release, company investor disclosure, or the source government's own trade-policy announcement) plus one corroborating source. Deals still at MOU/framework stage (not yet financially closed) are explicitly flagged as such rather than presented as landed investment.

paper.title: A game theory model of tariff rate quotas in international trade
paper.journal: Journal of Dynamics and Games, April 2025

game_theory_framework.model_summary: Models a tariff-rate quota (TRQ) as a Generalized Nash Equilibrium Problem (GNEP): multiple exporting firms compete for market access across multiple importing countries; each importing country sets a two-tier tariff schedule (a low tariff up to a volume threshold, a high tariff above it) and firms' quota constraints are shared, so one firm's allowed volume depends on how much market the others take. The paper proves existence/uniqueness of equilibrium under a monotonicity condition and solves it via variational inequality methods. Its numerical experiments show the core trade-off facing the importing country: raising the in-quota threshold increases government tariff revenue but compresses exporting-firm profits, while switching from a tariff-only regime to a strict quota redistributes market access rather than just repricing it.
game_theory_framework.why_it_applies_here: India's own trade policy has started using exactly this instrument -- a volume-capped quota paired with a differential tariff/duty regime -- rather than a flat tariff, in exactly the sectors this analysis covers. That makes the GNEP framing directly applicable: India (the importing country / quota-setter) is choosing thresholds and duty tiers, and the exporting-country supplier base (dominated in several sectors by one or two firms, e.g. China's GMCC in AC compressors) is playing a genuine multi-firm competition for the quota, not a simple price-taking response to a tariff.

illustrative_case.name: DPIIT Quality Control Order (QCO) amendment on AC compressors, May 2026
illustrative_case.mechanics: Import volumes capped at 30% of FY2025 import volume for rotary/AC compressors (<2TR) and 40% for reciprocating/refrigerator compressors, layered on top of existing BIS quality-certification requirements that already function as a non-tariff barrier.
illustrative_case.stated_intent: Explicit government framing that Chinese compressor manufacturers -- the market is dominated by GMCC (Guangdong Meizhi) -- 'cannot [continue selling into India] unless they build factories in India,' i.e. the quota is designed to force a build-vs-exit choice rather than simply raise the landed price.
illustrative_case.gnep_read: This is a textbook instance of the paper's two-tier TRQ: firms that stay under India's volume cap face the existing (lower) duty regime; firms that would exceed it face a binding non-tariff wall (the quota itself, not just a higher rate) rather than the 'high tariff above threshold' the paper models -- India has substituted an absolute quota for the high-tariff tier, which the paper's numerical experiments show produces a more direct market-access reallocation than a tariff-only TRQ (firms cannot simply pay their way past the threshold). Predicted equilibrium response, consistent with the paper: GMCC and other China-based compressor makers either (a) establish or expand India-based production (converting export volume into FDI, which is the explicit policy goal), or (b) cede share to whichever competitor -- domestic (Amber Enterprises, Epack) or third-country (Korean LG/Samsung-linked supply chains) -- can operate inside the cap.

second_instance.name: India's own steel safeguard duty, in force through 2028
second_instance.mechanics: 12% safeguard duty (stepping down to 11.5% then 11% over the period) applied to flat steel imports, explicitly targeting China, Japan, Korea, Vietnam and Nepal.
second_instance.strategic_tension_flag: This directly complicates the Specialty Steel country-strategy below: Japan and Korea are simultaneously (a) the two most credible FDI-source countries for specialty/electrical steel technology transfer (JFE, POSCO-JSW) and (b) named targets of India's own import-restricting safeguard duty. The GNEP framing predicts this is not a contradiction but the intended mechanism -- the duty is the 'high tariff' tier pushing Japanese/Korean capacity toward Indian joint ventures (which is exactly what JFE's Nashik acquisition and the POSCO-JSW JV represent) rather than continued arm's-length exports. A quota/duty aimed at a country is not inconsistent with courting that same country's FDI; in this model it is often the mechanism that produces the FDI.

official_tariff_timeline.note: Restricted to official government/court actions and published proclamations -- explicitly excludes news-cycle speculation, unconfirmed trade-deal 'frameworks,' and threatened-but-not-issued tariffs.

events.event: US 'reciprocal tariff' regime under IEEPA imposed and repeatedly revised on India, ranging 26% to 50% at various points across the year, including a punitive tier explicitly tied to India's continued purchase of Russian crude.

US Supreme Court strikes down the entire IEEPA-based reciprocal-tariff/emergency-tariff framework as exceeding executive authority. Every India-specific reciprocal-tariff rate issued under that framework is voided.

Replaced, as an interim measure, with a flat global 10% tariff surcharge under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 -- applies uniformly to all trading partners, not India-specific, and is capped in duration by statute.

The Section 122 flat 10% surcharge is set to expire by statutory limit (5 days after this report's retrieval date) unless separately extended or replaced -- the single most consequential near-term date in the current tariff picture.
A proposed 12.5% Section 301 tariff explicitly naming India (among other countries) is pending as the intended replacement mechanism once Section 122 lapses; not yet in force as of retrieval.
US Section 232 tariff of 50% on steel and aluminum imports, applied globally with no India-specific exemption negotiated.
US Section 232 tariff of 25% on semiconductors, narrowly scoped to AI-accelerator-class chips; India not specifically named.
US Section 232 tariff of 100% on patented/branded pharmaceuticals; generic drugs and biosimilars explicitly excluded 'at this time' -- directly relevant to India's generics-heavy pharma export base.

A tariff-rate-quota carve-out for Indian auto parts was proposed in a bilateral framework discussion but has NOT been published or implemented as an actual rate as of retrieval -- included here as a proposal under discussion, not an official action.
India-US Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA) negotiations remain unsigned as of retrieval, despite multiple negotiating rounds; no finalized market-access commitments to report on either side.

US-China tariffs remain separately in force at Section 301 levels (roughly 25%/7.5% across List tariff tiers) plus sectoral rates (100% on EVs, 50% on semiconductors, 50% on solar cells) -- this layer was not affected by the Supreme Court's IEEPA ruling, so the US-China tariff wall persists even as the US-India reciprocal-tariff layer was struck down.

EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) definitive period begins on schedule, covering iron/steel, aluminium, cement, fertilizer, hydrogen and electricity imports -- directly exposes India's ~$20.7bn HS72/73 steel export book.
India's own DPIIT Quality Control Order amendment imposes import volume quotas (30-40% of FY2025 volumes) on AC and refrigerator compressors -- see game-theory illustrative case above.
India's own steel safeguard duty (12% stepping down to 11%) remains in force against China, Japan, Korea, Vietnam and Nepal.

official_tariff_timeline.cross_cutting_observation: Because the entire 2025-era reciprocal-tariff schedule was voided by the Supreme Court, the widely-cited 'India lost competitiveness to Vietnam/Bangladesh because of a higher reciprocal tariff rate' narrative is currently MOOT -- Vietnam's 20% and Bangladesh's ~19% baseline rates were struck down in the same ruling, and all three (India, Vietnam, Bangladesh) currently face the same flat 10% Section 122 surcharge. Any sector strategy built on a presumed India-vs-Vietnam tariff gap needs to be re-checked once (if) the Section 301 replacement actually takes effect on 2026-07-24.

china_plus_one_honesty_flag.source: NITI Aayog Trade Watch Quarterly (India's own government think tank)
china_plus_one_honesty_flag.finding: NITI Aayog's own published assessment states China+1 diversification has had 'limited success so far' for India specifically -- Vietnam, Thailand, Cambodia and Malaysia have captured a larger share of the manufacturing relocation away from China than India has. This is the government's own admission, not an external critique, and should temper any country-strategy narrative that assumes India is the default China+1 beneficiary.

sectors.pli_status: strong -- PLI Electronics + India Semiconductor Mission (ISM) 2.0

target_countries.rationale: Process-technology and equipment partner, not primarily a capital source -- PSMC's technology-licensing role in the Tata Dholera fab is the model: Taiwan transfers fab process IP and know-how while India and other partners fund the capex.
target_countries.evidence: Tata Electronics-PSMC Dholera fab, Rs 91,000cr (~$11bn), trial production targeted Dec 2026.

target_countries.rationale: The only target country with a treaty-level government-to-government mechanism specifically for this sector.
target_countries.evidence: MeitY-METI Memorandum of Cooperation, July 2023, operationalized via the India-Japan Industrial Competitiveness Partnership (IJICP).

target_countries.rationale: Equipment and advanced-materials supply chain, framed under the bilateral technology-security dialogue rather than a manufacturing-investment mechanism.
target_countries.evidence: iCET (initiative on Critical and Emerging Technology) and the follow-on TRUST framework.

sectors.tariff_relevance: Section 232 semiconductor tariff (25%) is narrowly scoped to AI-accelerator chips and does not name India -- limited direct exposure for this sector's current PLI-covered product base.
flags: The Vedanta-Foxconn Dholera JV collapsed in July 2023 (Foxconn exited) -- do not cite it as active.

sectors.pli_status: strong -- PLI Specialty Steel, Rs 44,106cr committed capacity + Rs 11,887cr incentive outlay

target_countries.rationale: Direct technology-transfer route for electrical/specialty steel grades, including acquisition of an existing India-based asset rather than a greenfield JV.
target_countries.evidence: JFE Steel's technology partnership and acquisition of thyssenkrupp's Nashik electrical-steel business.

target_countries.rationale: The largest live specialty-steel FDI commitment in the sector, plus a fresh government-to-government mechanism opened in 2026.
target_countries.evidence: POSCO-JSW joint venture, $7.73bn; new Korea-India Steel Cooperation MOU, April 2026.

target_countries.rationale: European specialty-steel technology suppliers (Danieli, SMS group) sell process equipment and licenses into Indian expansions but are not equity FDI sources -- flagged explicitly as a different relationship type from Japan/Korea.
target_countries.evidence: Ongoing equipment-supply contracts tied to PLI-backed capacity expansions; no equity stake identified.

sectors.tariff_relevance: Direct tension with India's own steel safeguard duty (in force through 2028, targeting China/Japan/Korea/Vietnam/Nepal) and with the EU CBAM definitive period starting Jan 2026 on the export side -- see game_theory_framework.second_instance and cbam_export_risk in the companion sector file.
flags: Germany is exiting, not entering, this sector -- earlier drafts of this research incorrectly assumed a Germany target; thyssenkrupp's Nashik divestiture to JFE is an EXIT, not an investment.

sectors.pli_status: strong -- PLI Auto/Auto Components + Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery PLI

target_countries.rationale: The strongest and most diversified current relationship: equipment flowing into India's own battery-gigafactory buildout plus active cell-technology partnership talks, reinforced by an upgraded trade agreement.
target_countries.evidence: Equipment supply into Agratas' (Tata) gigafactory; LG Energy Solution-JSW cell-technology discussions; India-Korea CEPA upgrade negotiations.

target_countries.rationale: The largest single committed capital figures in the sector, plus the same IJICP government mechanism used for electronics.
target_countries.evidence: Toyota, $1.9bn; Suzuki, Rs 70,000cr (~$8.3bn at period-matched FY24-25 rate); IJICP.

non_target_flag.reason: A complication, not a target -- India's EV/battery buildout remains structurally dependent on Chinese graphite, lithium-compound and rare-earth supply even as India actively courts Korean and Japanese battery capital; this is an upstream-input dependency, not an FDI opportunity.

sectors.tariff_relevance: US Section 232 auto tariff (25%) is global, not India-specific; a proposed TRQ carve-out for Indian auto parts was discussed in Feb 2026 but has NOT been published or implemented as an actual rate.
flags: Tata's and Reliance's battery-technology partners are not yet confirmed in public disclosures -- do not cite specific cell-chemistry licensors for either group as settled.

sectors.pli_status: partial -- PLI Bulk Drugs (Rs 6,940cr) covers 41 of 53 identified critical-KSM/API dependencies, not the full list

target_countries.rationale: The strongest live driver, propelled by a US domestic policy shift that is pushing biologics/CDMO manufacturing away from China and toward alternative sourcing including India.
target_countries.evidence: BIOSECURE Act-driven CDMO relocation; Eli Lilly, $1bn; Amgen, $200m.

target_countries.rationale: A weak, adjacent-only relationship -- flagged honestly rather than inflated.
target_countries.evidence: Miltenyi Biotec has adjacent biotech activity in India but no confirmed bulk-drug-API-specific investment identified.

reversed_assumption_flag.correction: The Israel pharma narrative runs the OPPOSITE direction from what is commonly assumed: Sun Pharma (Indian) acquired Israel's Taro Pharmaceutical, i.e. Indian capital flowing INTO Israel, not Israeli FDI into India. Do not cite Israel as an FDI-source country for this sector.

sectors.tariff_relevance: US Section 232 pharma tariff is 100% but explicitly EXCLUDES generics and biosimilars 'at this time' -- a genuine strategic opening given India's generics-dominant export profile, worth flagging as the one clearly favorable current tariff carve-out in this entire analysis.
flags: China API dependency for Indian bulk-drug manufacturing is closer to ~74% by value for the most critical KSMs, not the commonly cited 65-70% range -- use the higher, sourced figure.

sectors.pli_status: strong -- PLI White Goods (ACs & LED), fully subscribed, 85 of 85 approved applicant slots filled

target_countries.rationale: The most active investor by count and by recurring commitment, spanning both the market leader and a compressor-specific supplier.
target_countries.evidence: Daikin, Rs 547cr + a further Rs 1,400cr expansion; Nidec/Embraco compressor manufacturing.

target_countries.rationale: Both major Korean appliance majors have live committed capital in this exact PLI window.
target_countries.evidence: Samsung, $205.7m; LG Electronics, additional committed capacity.

target_countries.rationale: A concentrated, component-specific relationship (compressors/controls) rather than a broad appliance-brand presence.
target_countries.evidence: Danfoss, Rs 500cr+ cumulative.

sectors.tariff_relevance: Directly implicated by the May 2026 DPIIT compressor QCO quota -- see game_theory_framework.illustrative_case; Japan (Nidec/Embraco) and Korea are the compressor-technology partners best positioned to absorb the quota-driven localization this policy is designed to force.

sectors.pli_status: none -- ground-floor opportunity, no existing scheme (Tier 1 in the original import-substitution framework: $14.2bn FY25-26 imports, +86.0% growth)

target_countries.rationale: The clearest sector-specific commitments identified, from two distinct Japanese majors, as part of a broader post-China-plus-one pivot toward India.
target_countries.evidence: Sumitomo Chemical and Mitsubishi Gas Chemical facility commitments.

target_countries.rationale: A real but general 'Focus on India' corporate strategy commitment, not an inorganic-chemicals-specific investment -- flagged as directional interest rather than a landed project.
target_countries.evidence: Lanxess, Rs 750cr cumulative investment under its stated India strategy; BASF and Evonik have broader India activity but nothing identified as inorganic-chemicals-specific.

sectors.tariff_relevance: No sector-specific tariff action identified in either direction as of retrieval.
flags: This sector has no PLI scheme at all -- any country-strategy pitch here is necessarily a 'why invest ahead of the incentive' pitch, not a 'come collect the subsidy' pitch, and should be framed that way.

sectors.pli_status: none -- ground-floor opportunity, no existing scheme ($22.2bn FY25-26 imports, +45.8% growth)

target_countries.rationale: The single largest headline commitment in this analysis by dollar value, but still at MOU/framework stage, not financial close -- flagged explicitly rather than presented as landed.
target_countries.evidence: Saudi Aramco / ADNOC-backed Ratnagiri Refinery & Petrochemicals (RRPCL), $44bn proposed, STALLED at MOU stage as of retrieval.

target_countries.rationale: A broad, non-petrochemical-specific capital commitment -- included as a directional signal, not a sector-specific project.
target_countries.evidence: Qatar's broader $10bn India investment commitment; no petrochemical-specific allocation confirmed.

target_countries.rationale: A specific, named but pre-final-investment-decision project.
target_countries.evidence: Mitsubishi Chemical MMA (methyl methacrylate) plant, pre-FID as of retrieval.

flags: The RRPCL project's $44bn figure has been publicly discussed for years without reaching financial close -- do not present it as committed capital in any pitch material without the MOU-stage caveat attached.

sectors.pli_status: none -- ground-floor opportunity, no existing scheme ($13.8bn FY25-26 imports, +80.9% growth)

target_countries.rationale: The only fully operating, financially-closed foreign manufacturing investment in this entire sector, plus a second live MRO/JV track.
target_countries.evidence: Tata-Airbus C295 final assembly line, Vadodara -- operating. Safran, EUR 200m+ MRO investment; BEL-Safran joint venture.

target_countries.rationale: The largest count of live engagements, spanning engine co-production (not yet signed), an engineering center, a manufacturing JV, and an already-high-localization existing program.
target_countries.evidence: GE-HAL F414 engine co-production -- discussed, NOT yet signed as of retrieval. Boeing, $200m+ India engineering center; Tata-Boeing joint venture; Lockheed Martin C-130J program already at 96% local content.

target_countries.rationale: A newly signed defense-adjacent joint venture, distinct from the civil-aviation relationships above.
target_countries.evidence: IAI (Israel Aerospace Industries)-DCX radar joint venture, signed May 2026.

sectors.tariff_relevance: No sector-specific tariff action identified; this sector's FDI story is defense/strategic-partnership-driven rather than tariff-driven.
flags: The GE-HAL F414 engine deal is frequently reported as done -- it is a discussed co-production arrangement, NOT a signed agreement, as of retrieval. Do not present it as committed.

sectors.pli_status: partial -- PLI Medical Devices, Rs 3,420cr outlay

target_countries.rationale: The most-cited landed investment in the sector, though the underlying commitment predates this PLI window -- flagged for vintage rather than presented as a fresh 2025-26 signal.
target_countries.evidence: Siemens Healthineers, EUR 160m -- a 2020-vintage commitment, still the reference investment cited for this sector, currently in production under the PLI umbrella.

target_countries.rationale: An R&D-specific commitment paired with a newly opened government-to-government research-cooperation mechanism.
target_countries.evidence: Olympus R&D center; new AMED-ICMR (Japan-India) Memorandum of Cooperation, August 2025.

target_countries.rationale: The two largest dollar commitments in the sector, one a direct manufacturer investment and one a joint-venture-structured deal.
target_countries.evidence: Medtronic, $350m; GE Healthcare-Wipro joint venture, Rs 8,000cr.

sectors.pli_status: mismatched -- PLI Textiles exists (Rs 10,683cr) but its scope targets man-made fibre (MMF) and technical textiles, structurally EXCLUDING the cotton-based apparel that makes up the declining export chapters (HS 61/62, -2.3% and -2.0% growth, per the original export_growth_tiers Tier 1 finding)
sectors.honest_finding: No credible FDI-source country was identified for this sector in this research pass. The dominant real capital movement here is Indian-owned capacity returning to India FROM Bangladesh (a reshoring/derisking move by Indian textile groups themselves, not inbound foreign direct investment), not a foreign country's capital entering India. Any country-strategy pitch for this sector would be fabricating a relationship that the evidence does not support.
sectors.tariff_relevance: This sector shares the same voided-reciprocal-tariff arc as every other Indian export chapter (see official_tariff_timeline.cross_cutting_observation) -- the widely assumed India-vs-Vietnam-vs-Bangladesh competitiveness gap from differential reciprocal tariffs is currently moot since all three now face the same flat 10% Section 122 surcharge.
flags: This is the one sector in the top-10 set where the honest answer is 'no FDI-country story exists yet' -- the policy fix here (per the original recommendation) is broadening PLI Textiles' scope to cotton apparel, not a country-targeting pitch.
